lines.text: *Full Reverse.* The title is the chapter's last act of physical violence — Lysanne Sutherland slamming a flatbed transporter into a guardian's body.
The epigraph.Narrating *forward*. *Collapse. That's what'll happen to the Northern Alliance on Sol.*

The bee-rhyme metaphor from chapter 20 returns in prose. *Three billion insects, realizing the lid closed. They were, once again, trapped in their jar. The watch.* Humanity is trapped on Earth in a grinding of life against life. Trapped in the watch. In time.

And the epigraph is the first time the book has explicitly called the encounter *First Contact.* The chapter is asserting — as an article of faith — that what happened in the Fold, what happens in the Cryo-Bay, what happened to Su, are all instances of the same event.

Open in Sector 4. Lysanne meets Oliver, Ian, and a woman Lysanne has not met before. *She wore a bright white lab coat and stood completely still, watching everything closely.* Ian introduces her. *Lysanne Sutherland, this is Joana. My wife.*
Lysanne decided she liked her immediately, which was unusual. Alliance on chemistry.

Oliver pitches the plan. *In two clicks, there'll be a major event. It'll be our only chance.* And the chapter does its most quietly subversive line in Lysanne's response — not in speech, but in inner-voice. *Our. When had it become *our*?*

Oliver's plan is named in three beats. *I'll go to the depot, get the Egg, and use it to disappear from the Grid. Permanently.* Then the second beat — *Maybe I'm catching a ride to the Delta.* And then the third — *Someone reached me. Through the civilian network. They know about the Egg. They're going to extract me during the Brisinger's approach window.*

The chapter is collapsing the resistance plot threads. The Stingray descending. Oliver disappearing from the Grid. Joana liberating Vilde from the cryo-bay. Lysanne taking him to the depot. Three operations, one window — *the Brisinger's approach* — and the chapter has just synchronized them.
The cryo-bay. Joana crawls through the shaft. *A row of twelve silent pods appeared before her.* She scans the nameplates. *L, T. S, K.*  *And At the end:* And the end. *V, A.*
*V, A.* Vilde, Astrid. *She had found her.*
*Unbidden, the liquid around Vilde's face rippled. A vibration reached Joana, traveling through the floor plates beneath her feet. The sound swelled.*

The *Brisinger* is descending. A *seismic event.* Literally. The colony's foundation is vibrating. And the chapter is — in the same sentence — telling the reader that the vibration is *finding* Vilde in cryo-gel.

Cut to the Industrial Perimeter. *Dawn over Mairee was a bruised purple with rivers of pale green phosphor.* The colony's dawns hare described in trauma colors. The *bruise* is the chapter's tonal signature for this scene.

And the chapter writes Oliver's interior life with restraint. *He'd told himself he just needed to close the file. But the truth was a cold knot in his stomach. That if he hadn't finished it then, he might never get to.*
Leaving his design behind — marks, for Oliver, a new beginning.

Lysanne checks him. *Go get the Egg and disappear before they catch you. No distractions.* He replies. *No detours.* The chapter is having them rehearse the script aloud — not because they need to, but because *saying it makes it real.* The chapter is writing the operational call-and-response of two people about to commit a crime.

And then the chapter delivers the violence the reader has been bracing for. *Two guardians stepped out from behind the transporter.* The older one — *a canyon of cynical wrinkles.* The younger one — *not much older than Oliver, twitched.* The chapter is staging the colony's worst self in two body types.
Older guardian: bored predator with rank and license. Younger guardian: *clearly high on Zups.*
*Three thousand of us. Most men. You know how many women your age? Not many. No resupply. So, you can't waste a chance like this. More precious than water. More precious than fuel.*

The chapter has just compressed Margaret Atwood's *Handmaid's Tale* into a paragraph. *Demographic scarcity as license for sexual coercion.* The chapter does not editorialize. It just lets him say it. *Every able body is a colony asset. And isn't she a fine one.*
*The Centurion said so.* That's the chapter's institutional pin. The older guardian is *quoting Jax.*
*The younger guardian said nothing. His eyes went somewhere above the tarp. Somewhere far away.* Another portrait of *bystander dissociation.*

Compare chapter 18. Samantha standing outside the nursery office while Jax assaulted Zoë. *Inner-voice italicised thoughts — Go in. Stop him. If you don't move now…* The chapter is staging the *same* bystander figure in a different uniform. The colony's complicity is structural and repeats.

And then Ian Perera intervenes. *Let them go.* In one smooth sweep — pulling the guardian's pistol from its mag holster — and the instant failure. All with a single technical line. *That pistol is print-protected, Engineer.*
Biometric authorization. Ian's authentication does not match the firearm. *Print-protected* — fingerprint, palm-print, whatever the Grid uses.

And Nyland then writes the fight in pure stage-direction. *Ian swung the pistol as a club. The guardian caught his wrist, twisted, and drove the stick into Ian's side.* No interiority. The chapter is in *combat tempo.* The reader is supposed to be panting.

Oliver and Lysanne join in. Oliver with the rucksack. Lysanne with the kick to the knee. Oliver with the *multi-tool blade* — and then *Oliver darted forward and stabbed the guardian in the thigh.* The chapter has been signalling Oliver as a *gentle* character for twenty chapters. The stab announcement that he is no longer that.

And then the older guardian fires during the struggle for the gun. *Both men dropped to the ground.* and *for a heartbeat, neither moved* — followed by the guardian raising his weapon *the red targeting laser shaking, and started moving toward Ian to finish him off.*
And Lysanne — *Lysanne, bless her, who had spent the chapter cynical, deflecting, sighing* — *didn't hesitate. She screamed with primal rage and slammed the throttle full reverse.*
